item.title: Confused
item.content:encoded:
+ show block
– hide block

spoilers?
So, I may have missed something, but how did the Foundation know to use the altar in the first place? Before figuring out the communication bit?

item.title: Overhaul
item.content:encoded:
Check out that beauty! Look at all the new content. Smell the quality. This is the article you probably wanted from the start, but every day is a learning experience.

Time to consider rescinding that downvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is correct. Because of the altar's anomalous connection with death, imprinted informational constructs act like souls transversing the viel, with the altar being the focal point. They can essentially activate it by interacting with it through a live video feed of the altar.

MacLaughlin states that the D-Class in the last log is an informational construct. Implying that the entire dimension is made of information in some way. It explains why they don't starve to death.

Basically "souls" are made of raw data.

I added a footnote to perhaps help explain this a little, but the point is that the Foundation can't possibly know for certain how the altar works.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love this article, definite upvote for me! Specifically enjoyed MacLaughlin sending the D back. There is, however, one thing that seems inconsistent or omitted:

Members of the MTF omega zero are all informational constructs already, and according to the article can "[…] freely travel using SCP-2759-1's anomalous properties using an unknown vector." (the double "using" there feels odd to me but I'm not a native english speaker).

It was, and to an extend still is, unclear to me, how they were trying to get that MTF gear to transfer without someone dying on -1, unless the MTF omega 0 guys can initiate the transfer the same was as dying does. The way I understand it, MacLaughlin was only able to "die" that way because, in -E1, he was corporeal, unlike in our world.

Maybe I'm just misunderstanding or missing something. In any case, I very much enjoyed the connection to MTF omega zero :)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Noting that because of the float/width div stuff on the main chunk of this SCP, the wikiwalk module gets messed up when you open the collapsible and is crammed to the right.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Erm, I'm going to have to echo everybody above's sentiments. You looped me in with the UE and the Description, linked me in further with the first log, and completely broke me off at the second log.

No voting for no. This is an OK skip. It could be great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Show
- Hide

Thinking about it more, I think the problem is that you're trying to do too much here. You've got the ISA artcile, you've got the structure and the logs of the other planet, which are very interesting in and of themselves. Then you've got this story based on half-dead MTF who are contemplating what its like to live forever, what its like to die repeatedly, and they didn't even die in or because of this skip, but in some previous tale somewhere. You're using this skip to tell the end of their story, but we don't know the beginning of their story without reading something else.

Skips should be VERY self-contained in my opinion. For instance, I shouldn't need to know who Dr. Wondertainment is before reading a skip that references Dr. Wondertainment. The skip should provide any relevant information. Which isn't to say a skip couldn't be -enhanced- by knowing the backstory of the GOI/characters involved, but it shouldn't be necessary.

I would honestly have separated this out into a skip and a tale, crosslinking them by putting a link in one of the names. If you rewrote some of the meat (but not ALL the reveals) of the exploration log as recorded by D-Class, or generic MTF, and then maybe included a link at the bottom of the article saying, "Additional exploration logs can be found here," and that's where your story of undead MTF exploring this thing and contemplating their existence, then I think this would be more successful.

Obviously this skip is in no danger of being voted off the site, but I really think this could be a SUPER successful skip, like 100+ easily, if not for these problems.

But as always, this is just one man's opinion, take it with a grain of salt.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks, I'll have to put some thought into how to introduce them.

Feel free to contribute any ideas you may have

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just want to add my voice to what other people I have been saying: I was totally smitten and enraptured by this until McLaughlin came in. You wrote something beautiful and mysterious and intricate, but then suddenly the skip becomes something else. Also, I couldn't quite motivate myself to read the journal —
even having loved most of the skip, I don't know that character, and because of that I don't care what happens to him, I roughly understand the nature of the anomaly at that point, so there is not enough tension/emotional investment for me to plunge into a long tale.

I hope you make some revisions to it — right now, its a good SCP but it has the potential to be amazing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've thought about the article at the beginning and I think it works because it is limited to Level 5, it is just made to look like another article that people would pass off for nothing. Obviously, though, it was placed there on purpose.

Obviously the whole MTF Omega-Zero thing works best if having read 2111.

I did link it on first mention of them. Idk how else to introduce them given they don't have a slot on the Mobile Task Forces page and I don't want to go off on a tangent on how they were created and why they can't die, etc etc, because that's all covered in the other skip.

If you have any ideas please let me know!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
No vote for now. Will revisit later.

+ Show
- Hide

I'm usually not a fan of long skips, but this kept me interested… all up until about the second half of the second exploration log, where I lost interest. I really had to force my way through the accompanying personal log. I don't know any of these characters, I don't know their backstory, I just clicked on this skip and it should work on its own. Which it does, up to that point. Then there's a lot of emotional exposition that doesn't work for me, because, again I don't know these characters, I'm not sure why or how they're ghosts or immortal or whatever, so a lot of that stuff falls flat.

I'm also not sure that you need the ISA article at the beginning. I guess it worked to pique my interest, but really, it adds nothing new to the article and doesn't say or give anything that's not in the SCP document itself.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The place instills fear, it's all the coghaz talking. I'll take another look at the dialogue, thanks.

That's usually one of my strong suits, so it's a shame. Also, the camera doesn't start working again, the D is just unresponsive.

Any recommendations on where and what I should change? I heavily respect your opinion :p

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Upvoted with reservations. I love Sumer and Ara Orun, but the former felt underused. A lot of the same alt dimension skip cliches we usually see (like the camera mysteriously working again long after it originally failed) were present but packaged in Sumerian paint. It would have been more interesting if it were more distinctly Sumerian.

The dialogue is a bit hamfisted. Asking if he’s going to die conveys his attitude quite well, going on to describe the place in melodramatic terms just feels blunt in a bad way. We get it, the place is spooky.

The piece could def be something great, but it’s not quite there atm.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this one. shout out to all my homies on the irc that helped with feedback

spoilers
– hide block

I based this around some various Sumerian texts I've been reading.

There is a lot of subtext here and in the personal log.

Since I had to cut it down, I'll put the fullish story here:

Thousands of years ago, a civilization of hyperadvanced humans thrived on a planet orbiting the star Nibiru. A wrathful god attempted to wipe them out by causing another planet to collide with theirs.
Using their anomalous, advanced technologies, they created fleets of ships made of clay and stone (the temples) in order to transport large amounts of people off their planet and to another dimension. They ultimately failed in doing so, and only a handful of people made it through. They left them with the altars, which would let them come back if they could somehow find a way to save their homeworld.

Sumerian mythology states that there were seven gods and fifty lesser gods. The burden of existence was placed on humans upon their creation, in order to lessen the toll it placed on the gods themselves
Anyway, the lesser gods, The Annunaki, blessed fifty individuals with immortality, because death was considered the ultimate punishment in Sumerian religion. There is no heaven or hell, there is only suffering after death, above and below.

Fast forward to the present, and another ship randomly appears in White Sands. The thing is tied to their gods, their dimension, and shadows of the dead are occassionally seen.
In Sumerian lore, the underworld denies entry to specific people and specific items. Weapons, articles of clothing and such were cast out.

They discover that on a rare occasion, useful items can cross over.
Around this time, Omega-Zero, who are constructs, disembodied consciousness of the once-alive, plagued to exist forever and trapped within the foundation databases, came across this article and attempted to interact with the altar. Due to the anomalous nature of the technology, their consciousness can traverse the dimensions freely. In the afterlife, they possess their physical forms. Still dead, mind you. That's why you never see them return.
The alternate destinations reference the Garden of the Gods, the Underworld and the Homeworld, all connected by a single point.

So, they investigate. Larenz either goes mad or makes a realization that he is one of the fifty. After Fontaine is annihilated, he seeks the same fate, because many of them actually want to die. (Which I felt was a good spin on the MTFs constantly dying trope.)

And of course, there is O5-11 and his hidden agenda, which is his quest for immortality. It's a continuation of the skip where he dies and experiences years of sustained agony. Obviously his intent was to find a way to preserve himself forever.

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Great_Ziggurat_of_Ur.JPG

https://www.flickr.com/photos/hanzler666/6831566115/in/photolist-bpFyAF-6WVShD-N8ZcUf-HCQoK-7zeeCt-gVJsAN-bxy31B-5iodym-23fThzu-f9DYR7-23V9ZbG-dkBqmK-A69Njj-9T5brJ-cHg1Hf-Uqfjno-39MJSr-9T2njB-fQsXrK-bq2BNo-9ChZvg-mR35Jp-V333Vb-e66GDJ-Jpxezu-HCBYc3-39MK4P-5CZRUL-fyHyPL-cfB1QC-cHg1Mj-5CVz9r-8vBdaC-m1hwfS-bRc78B-2bBnLWt-88UQLY-9TKuQx-aYTgq4-9aEr8v-3gFz-oK1ZrB-2eiw5aY-au4amg-jj7Bd4-qu7NUY-jAEqeR-hVDg17-93Fwe5-7hJz2r
